description: Add plugin management APIs (search, install, update, remove) to the MCP server with JSON-RPC endpoints accessible from the side panel, plus comprehensive E2E test coverage at the API level.

userStories.title: Plugin search and install APIs on MCP server
userStories.description: As a side panel user, I want to search the npm registry for available plugins and install them directly from the UI so that I can discover and add plugins without using the command line.
acceptanceCriteria: New JSON-RPC method `plugin.search` accepts `{ query?: string }` params via the extension WebSocket, queries the npm registry for `keywords:opentabs-plugin` packages, and returns `{ results: Array<{ name, description, version, author, isOfficial }> }` with up to 20 results

New JSON-RPC method `plugin.install` accepts `{ name: string }` params via the extension WebSocket, normalizes shorthand names (e.g. 'slack' -> 'opentabs-plugin-slack'), runs `npm install -g <package>`, triggers rediscovery, and returns `{ ok: true, plugin: { name, displayName, version, toolCount } }` on success

plugin.install returns structured JSON-RPC errors: -32602 for invalid/missing params, -32603 for npm install failure (with stderr in error data), -32603 for package not being a valid opentabs plugin after install
plugin.search returns structured JSON-RPC errors: -32603 for npm registry unreachable, -32603 with retryAfterMs for rate limiting (HTTP 429)
Both methods require a JSON-RPC id (request/response pattern) — notifications without id are rejected
plugin.install sends a `plugins.changed` notification to the side panel after successful install so the UI refreshes
The side panel can already receive responses to JSON-RPC requests (existing bridge.ts sendRequest pattern) — the new methods follow the same pattern and need no extension-side changes
Both methods are added to the extension-protocol.ts handleExtensionMessage dispatch and follow existing patterns (validate params, send response via sendToExtension)
npm install subprocess has a 60-second timeout to prevent hanging on network issues
plugin.install rejects attempts to install packages that don't match the opentabs-plugin-* or @scope/opentabs-plugin-* naming convention with a clear error
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
userStories.notes:
Implementation locations:

1. `platform/mcp-server/src/plugin-management.ts` — New module for all plugin management logic. Contains:
   - `searchNpmPlugins(query?)`: Fetches from `https://registry.npmjs.org/-/v1/search` with `keywords:opentabs-plugin` + optional query, size=20. Maps results to `{ name, description, version, author, isOfficial }`. Handles 429 (rate limit) and network errors.
   - `installPlugin(name, state, sessionServers, transports)`: Normalizes name → full npm package name. Spawns `npm install -g <pkg>` with 60s timeout via AbortSignal. On success, calls `performConfigReload(state, sessionServers, transports)` to trigger rediscovery. Returns installed plugin info from the refreshed registry. Sends `plugins.changed` notification to extension.
   - `normalizePluginName(name)`: Same logic as CLI — if name starts with `@` or `opentabs-plugin-`, pass through; else prepend `opentabs-plugin-`.
   - `isValidPluginPackageName(name)`: Validates the normalized name matches `opentabs-plugin-*` or `@scope/opentabs-plugin-*` pattern.

2. `platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts` — Add handlers in `handleExtensionMessage()`:
   - After the existing `config.setAllToolsEnabled` block (~line 599), add:
     ```
     if (method === 'plugin.search' && id !== undefined) { handlePluginSearch(state, params, id); return; }
     if (method === 'plugin.install' && id !== undefined) { handlePluginInstall(state, params, id, callbacks); return; }
     ```
   - The handlers should import from plugin-management.ts and wrap results in JSON-RPC responses.
   - IMPORTANT: `handlePluginInstall` needs access to `sessionServers` and `transports` for rediscovery. Pass these through the McpCallbacks interface by adding: `onPluginInstall: (name: string) => Promise<{ ok: boolean; plugin?: {...}; error?: string }>` — or alternatively, store references to sessionServers/transports in the plugin-management module. The cleanest approach: extend `McpCallbacks` with `onReload: () => Promise<{ plugins: number; durationMs: number }>` that calls `performConfigReload()`, and have the handler use that.

3. `platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.ts` — In `createMcpCallbacks()` (~line 51), add the `onReload` callback:
   ```
   onReload: () => performConfigReload(state, sessionServers, transports),
   ```

4. Follow existing error response patterns: `sendToExtension(state, { jsonrpc: '2.0', error: { code: -32602, message: '...' }, id })` for param errors, code -32603 for internal errors. Include `data: { stderr: '...' }` for npm failures so the side panel can show useful error details.

5. Use `platformExec('npm')` from `@opentabs-dev/shared` for cross-platform npm execution (same as CLI does in platform/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts:91).

6. The `plugins.changed` notification is already handled by the extension message-router.ts (in SIDE_PANEL_METHODS set) and the side panel App.tsx (triggers loadPlugins refresh). Just send: `sendToExtension(state, { jsonrpc: '2.0', method: 'plugins.changed', params: {} })`.

userStories.title: Plugin update and remove APIs on MCP server
userStories.description: As a side panel user, I want to update outdated plugins and remove installed plugins from the UI so that I can manage the full plugin lifecycle without using the command line.
acceptanceCriteria: New JSON-RPC method `plugin.update` (note: distinct from the existing `plugin.update` notification used for file-watcher IIFE updates — this is a request with an id) accepts `{ name: string }` params, runs `npm update -g <package>`, triggers rediscovery, and returns `{ ok: true, plugin: { name, displayName, version, toolCount } }` on success
To disambiguate from the existing `plugin.update` notification (no id, used by file watcher), the new method is named `plugin.updateFromRegistry` to avoid confusion

New JSON-RPC method `plugin.remove` accepts `{ name: string }` params, runs `npm uninstall -g <package>`, removes matching entries from localPlugins in config.json, triggers rediscovery, sends `plugin.uninstall` to the extension to clean up adapters, and returns `{ ok: true }` on success
plugin.updateFromRegistry returns structured JSON-RPC errors: -32602 for invalid params, -32603 for npm update failure (with stderr in error data), -32602 if the plugin is not currently installed

plugin.remove returns structured JSON-RPC errors: -32602 for invalid params, -32603 for npm uninstall failure, -32602 if the plugin is not currently installed (for npm plugins) or not found in localPlugins (for local plugins)
plugin.remove handles both npm-installed and local plugins: for npm plugins it runs npm uninstall -g; for local plugins it removes the entry from config.json localPlugins array
Both methods send `plugins.changed` notification to the side panel after successful operations

New JSON-RPC method `plugin.checkUpdates` accepts `{}` params, checks all npm-installed plugins for updates (using the existing version-check.ts logic), and returns `{ outdatedPlugins: Array<{ name, currentVersion, latestVersion, updateCommand }> }`
Both npm subprocesses (update, uninstall) have a 60-second timeout
plugin.remove for local plugins persists the updated config via the existing config write mutex pattern (saveConfig or read-modify-write like saveToolConfig)
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
userStories.notes:
Implementation locations:

1. `platform/mcp-server/src/plugin-management.ts` — Add to the module created in US-001:
   - `updatePlugin(name, state, callbacks)`: Normalizes name. Verifies plugin exists in registry (by checking state.registry.plugins or npm package name match). Spawns `npm update -g <pkg>` with 60s timeout. On success, calls `callbacks.onReload()` to rediscover. Returns updated plugin info from refreshed registry.
   - `removePlugin(name, state, callbacks)`: Normalizes name. Determines if the plugin is npm-installed or local (check state.registry.plugins for the plugin and inspect its `source` field). For npm: runs `npm uninstall -g <pkg>`. For local: reads config.json, filters out the matching localPlugins entry (match by resolving each local path and comparing package.json name), writes updated config. In both cases: calls `callbacks.onReload()`, then sends `plugin.uninstall` notification to extension to clean up adapters.
   - `checkPluginUpdates(state)`: Calls the existing `checkForUpdates(state)` from version-check.ts, then returns `state.outdatedPlugins`. Alternatively, re-implement inline to return results directly without modifying state. The existing `checkForUpdates` writes to `state.outdatedPlugins` and returns void — just call it and read the result.

2. `platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts` — Add handlers after US-001's additions:
   ```
   if (method === 'plugin.updateFromRegistry' && id !== undefined) { handlePluginUpdateFromRegistry(state, params, id, callbacks); return; }
   if (method === 'plugin.remove' && id !== undefined) { handlePluginRemove(state, params, id, callbacks); return; }
   if (method === 'plugin.checkUpdates' && id !== undefined) { handlePluginCheckUpdates(state, id); return; }
   ```

3. For `plugin.remove` with local plugins, follow the config write pattern from `saveToolConfig` in config.ts:
   - Read config from disk via readConfigWithRetry
   - Filter out matching localPlugins entries
   - Write back atomically via the configWriteMutex
   - Export a new `removeLocalPlugin(state, pluginName)` from config.ts or keep it in plugin-management.ts

4. The `plugin.uninstall` notification to the extension is already implemented in message-router.ts (known-methods.ts has it in DISPATCH_METHODS, message-router.ts has a handler). The server just needs to send: `sendToExtension(state, { jsonrpc: '2.0', method: 'plugin.uninstall', params: { name: pluginName }, id: getNextRequestId() })`.

5. IMPORTANT naming collision: The existing `plugin.update` in the codebase is a server→extension notification (no id) used by the file watcher to push updated adapter IIFEs. The new update-from-registry endpoint must use a different name: `plugin.updateFromRegistry`. This is critical — using the same method name would confuse the extension's message router.

6. For the `callbacks.onReload` approach: extend `McpCallbacks` interface with `onReload: () => Promise<{ plugins: number; durationMs: number }>`. Set it in http-routes.ts createMcpCallbacks to call `performConfigReload(state, sessionServers, transports)`. This keeps the plugin-management module decoupled from reload internals.

userStories.title: E2E tests for plugin management APIs
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want comprehensive E2E tests for all plugin management APIs so that we can verify the full lifecycle works correctly and catch regressions.
acceptanceCriteria: New test file `e2e/plugin-management.e2e.ts` with tests exercising all plugin management JSON-RPC methods at the API level

Test: `plugin.search` returns results when querying npm registry (use a known package name or broad keyword). Verify response shape: `{ results: Array<{ name, description, version, author, isOfficial }> }`
Test: `plugin.search` with no query returns results (all opentabs plugins). Verify response is an array
Test: `plugin.search` returns structured error for invalid params (e.g. params.query is a number instead of string)
Test: `plugin.install` with invalid params returns -32602 error. Test with missing name, name as number, empty string
Test: `plugin.install` with a non-existent package returns -32603 error with stderr data
Test: `plugin.install` with a name that violates naming convention returns -32602 error
Test: `plugin.remove` with invalid params returns -32602 error
Test: `plugin.remove` with a non-installed plugin returns -32602 error
Test: `plugin.updateFromRegistry` with invalid params returns -32602 error
Test: `plugin.updateFromRegistry` with a non-installed plugin returns -32602 error
Test: `plugin.checkUpdates` returns `{ outdatedPlugins: [...] }` shape (may be empty array if nothing is outdated)
Test: All methods reject when called as notifications (no id) — verify no response is sent (or method-not-found if applicable)
Test: Authentication — all methods require a valid WebSocket connection (they go through the extension protocol, not HTTP directly). Verify they work through the standard fixture WebSocket path
Tests use the existing E2E fixture infrastructure: mcpServer fixture for server lifecycle, direct WebSocket connection for JSON-RPC, or the mcpClient fixture where appropriate

Tests that actually install/remove npm packages use real npm operations with a test package. Create a minimal publishable test plugin tarball OR use the existing e2e-test plugin path. ALTERNATIVE: For install/remove integration tests that need real npm, create a local tarball with `npm pack` from the e2e-test plugin dir and install from the tarball file path — this avoids network dependency on npm registry. The test can `npm pack` the e2e-test plugin, then `npm install -g ./path-to-tarball.tgz`
Tests that exercise plugin.remove for local plugins: create a temporary local plugin via `createMinimalPlugin()`, add it to config, reload, then remove it and verify it's gone from the registry
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test:e2e passes (including the new tests)
